did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost, Logistic Regression) and neural networks (Pattern Recognition Neural Network, Feed-Forward Neural Network) for the binary classification of skin lesions (nevus vs. melanoma) using morpho-granulometry features derived from color clusters.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the extraction of morpho-granulometry features from skin lesion images and their classification using machine learning (Random Forest, XGBoost) and neural networks (PRNN, Feed-Forward, etc.) across three databases (Med-Node, PH2, 7-Point). It reports specific accuracy and F1-score results, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Classification of skin lesions using morpho-granulometry features derived from color clusters
journal: 2024 28th International Conference on System Theory, Control and Computing (ICSTCC)
brief_summary: This study addresses the binary classification of skin lesions, specifically distinguishing between nevus and melanoma, using morpho-granulometry features derived from color clusters. The authors utilized three public datasets (Med-Node, PH2, and 7-Point) and applied preprocessing steps including the Dull Razor filter and Local Graph Cut segmentation. To select relevant features, machine learning classifiers Random Forest and XGBoost were employed to filter out irrelevant color clusters before feeding them into neural networks. The classification performance was evaluated using three neural networks: Pattern Recognition Neural Network (PRNN), Feed-Forward Neural Network, and Logistic Regression. The results indicated that the Pattern Recognition Neural Network achieved the highest accuracy of 0.923 and an F1-score of 0.876 on the PH2 dataset, while Logistic Regression was identified as the best overall classifier for interpretable classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task (nevus vs. melanoma). Sample images from the 7-Point, PH2, and Med-Node datasets are shown in Figure 1.

One of the two classes in the binary classification task (nevus vs. melanoma). Described as a type of skin cancer that can be fatal. Sample images from the 7-Point, PH2, and Med-Node datasets are shown in Figure 1.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into nevus (benign) and melanoma (malignant) classes
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'binary classification of nevus vs. melanoma'. It uses machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost) for feature selection and neural networks (PRNN, Feed-Forward, Logistic Regression) for the final classification task. Performance is evaluated using accuracy and F1-score derived from confusion matrices (TP, FP, FN, TN).

datasets_used.name: Med-Node
datasets_used.description: A computer-assisted melanoma diagnosis system using non-dermoscopic images, cited as reference [14] in the paper. It is one of the three free image databases used to test the methodology.

use.training: Used for feature extraction and classification evaluation; specific split not reported.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking, cited as reference [13] in the paper. It is one of the three free image databases used to test the methodology.

use.training: Used for feature extraction and classification evaluation; specific split not reported.
use.testing: Used for classification evaluation; reported best accuracy of 0.923 and F1-score of 0.876 with PRNN.

datasets_used.name: 7-Point
datasets_used.description: A dataset based on the seven-point checklist for skin lesion classification, cited as reference [12] in the paper. It is one of the three free image databases used to test the methodology.

use.training: Used for feature extraction and classification evaluation; specific split not reported.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Suggested as a solution to the overfitting issue in the neural network classification phase.
Used for interpretable classification; validated using a 10-fold cross-validation algorithm.

statistical_methods_used.method: Confusion matrix metrics (Accuracy, F1-score, Precision, Recall)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to assess and determine the optimal model based on performance and to calculate the final feature based on recall and precision.

statistical_methods_used.method: Thresholding based on mean feature importance
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for feature selection; a feature is considered important if its value exceeds the mean of all features belonging to the same class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

PH2.notes: The Pattern Recognition Neural Network (PRNN) provided the highest accuracy of 0.923 and an F1-score of 0.876 on the PH2 database. The results for PRNN were superior when features were taken from the PH2 database, with accuracy and F1-score reaching more than 92% and 87%, respectively. Logistic Regression (LR) was identified as the best overall classifier for interpretable classification.

Med-Node.notes: Results are averages of three runs using five-fold cross-validation. All neural networks fed with the whole set of features had outcomes in terms of accuracy under 70%.

7-Point.notes: Results are averages of three runs using five-fold cross-validation.

Logistic Regression (LR).notes: Logistic Regression with Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (BFGS) optimization and a 10-fold cross-validation algorithm was used for interpretable classification and provided a score of 91.81%.

architecture_used.main_model: Three neural networks: Pattern Recognition Neural Network (PRNN), Cascade-forward Neural Network, and Feed-Forward Neural Network; Logistic Regression (LR) classifier.

training_details.feature_selection_method: Thresholding based on mean feature importance
training_details.validation_strategy: Five-fold cross-validation for NNs; 10-fold cross-validation for LR

preprocessing_techniques: Dull Razor filter applied to remove unwanted particles and hair from skin lesions
Local Graph Cut (LGC) segmentation technique used to separate the region of interest (lesion area) from the background
Color clustering into 23 color clusters based on RGB intensity ranges (Seidenari et al. method) prior to feature extraction
conclusions: The study successfully accomplished the binary classification of nevus vs. melanoma using morpho-granulometry features derived from color clusters.
The Pattern Recognition Neural Network (PRNN) achieved the highest performance on the PH2 dataset, with an accuracy of 0.923 and an F1-score of 0.876.

Logistic Regression (LR) with Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (BFGS) optimization and 10-fold cross-validation was identified as the best classifier for interpretable classification, achieving a score of 91.81%.
Random Forest (RF) and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) classifiers proved effective for feature selection, demonstrating that not all color clusters participate in classifying skin lesions.
Feeding neural networks with the whole set of features resulted in accuracy outcomes under 70%, whereas using selected features improved performance significantly.
The morpho-granulometry features, specifically Mg1, allowed for the evaluation of pattern texture distribution in color cluster features.
The study acknowledges limitations related to the limited number of images in the three used datasets (Med-Node, PH2, and 7-Point).
Future work will focus on creating hybrid texture features to find the best combination for skin lesion classification.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant limitation regarding the limited number of images in the three used datasets (Med-Node, PH2, and 7-Point), which may influence outcomes and conclusions.
The paper does not report external validation; results are confined to three specific public databases without testing on independent, unseen clinical cohorts.
Feeding neural networks with the whole set of features resulted in poor accuracy (under 70%), highlighting a risk of overfitting or noise interference without the proposed feature selection step.

The best performing model (PRNN) achieved high accuracy (0.923) only on the PH2 dataset, while performance was lower on Med-Node (0.6937) and 7-Point (0.753), suggesting potential dataset bias or variability in feature generalizability.

Logistic Regression was identified as the best classifier for interpretable classification (score 0.9181), but the paper does not provide detailed statistical significance tests or confidence intervals for the comparisons between models.

The methodology relies on preprocessing steps (Dull Razor filter, Local Graph Cut) and color clustering that may not generalize well to images with different acquisition qualities or artifacts not present in the benchmark datasets.
